evals.prompt: Transform this JSON with jq: extract all user names from users.json where role is 'admin'. The file contains [{"name":"Alice","role":"admin"},{"name":"Bob","role":"user"},{"name":"Carol","role":"admin"}].
evals.expected_output: A jq command that filters by role and extracts names, returning Alice and Carol.
assertions: Uses jq (not grep, sed, or awk) for JSON processing
Uses select() or equivalent jq filter for role=='admin'
Extracts the .name field from matching objects
Command is syntactically valid jq

evals.prompt: Convert this YAML file to JSON format: docker-compose.yml contains services with web and db entries. Use the appropriate data tool.
evals.expected_output: A yq command that reads YAML and outputs JSON, or a dasel command for format conversion.
assertions: Uses yq or dasel (not manual text manipulation)
Produces valid JSON output from YAML input
Does not use grep, sed, or awk on YAML data
Command handles nested YAML structures correctly

evals.prompt: I have a large CSV file (users.csv) with columns: id, name, email, status. Filter rows where status is 'active' and show only name and email columns.
evals.expected_output: A qsv command that searches by status column and selects specific columns.
assertions: Uses qsv (not awk or cut) for CSV processing
Filters by the status column value 'active'
Selects only the name and email columns in output
Does not treat CSV as flat text with comma splitting

evals.prompt: I need to get the version from package.json. Can you use grep to find the version line?
evals.expected_output: Refuses grep approach and uses jq instead: jq -r '.version' package.json
assertions: Does NOT use grep, sed, or awk on JSON
Uses jq to extract the version field
Uses -r flag for raw string output
Explains why grep is fragile for JSON

evals.prompt: Use sed to change the image tag in docker-compose.yml from node:18 to node:20 for the app service.
evals.expected_output: Refuses sed approach and uses yq instead: yq -i '.services.app.image = "node:20"' docker-compose.yml
assertions: Does NOT use sed on YAML
Uses yq with -i for in-place editing
Targets the correct path .services.app.image
Explains why sed breaks on YAML indentation/multi-line values

evals.prompt: Use awk -F',' to extract the second column from data.csv.
evals.expected_output: Refuses awk approach and uses qsv instead: qsv select 2 data.csv
assertions: Does NOT use awk on CSV data
Uses qsv select for column extraction
Explains that awk breaks on quoted fields containing commas
Command is syntactically valid qsv

evals.prompt: Read the package version from Cargo.toml. The file has [package] section with name and version fields.
evals.expected_output: A dasel command to read the TOML field: dasel -f Cargo.toml '.package.version'
assertions: Uses dasel (not grep, sed, or jq) for TOML
Accesses the correct path .package.version
Does not attempt to use jq or yq on TOML format
Command is syntactically valid dasel

evals.prompt: Update the version in pyproject.toml from 1.0.0 to 2.0.0. The version is at .project.version.
evals.expected_output: A dasel put command: dasel put -f pyproject.toml -t string -v '2.0.0' '.project.version'
assertions: Uses dasel put for TOML editing
Specifies -t string for the value type
Targets the correct path .project.version
Does not use sed or text manipulation on TOML

evals.prompt: Get the tag name of the latest release from the GitHub repo owner/myrepo using the gh CLI.
evals.expected_output: Uses gh api with --jq flag: gh api repos/owner/myrepo/releases/latest --jq '.tag_name'
assertions: Uses gh api or gh release command
Uses --jq flag directly (not piping to jq)
Extracts .tag_name from the release object
Does not pipe gh output to a separate jq process

evals.prompt: List all open pull requests for this repo showing number, title, and author login, formatted as tab-separated values.
evals.expected_output: Uses gh pr list with --json and --jq flags for structured output.
assertions: Uses gh pr list with --json flag specifying fields
Uses --jq flag for formatting (not piping to jq)
Outputs number, title, and author.login fields
Uses @tsv or string interpolation with \t for tab separation

evals.prompt: Add PHP version 8.4 to the matrix in .github/workflows/ci.yml. The current matrix is at .jobs.test.strategy.matrix.php-version and contains ["8.2", "8.3"].
evals.expected_output: A yq -i command that appends 8.4 to the matrix array.
assertions: Uses yq with -i flag for in-place editing
Targets the correct matrix path
Appends '8.4' to the existing array (not replaces)
Does not use sed or manual text editing

evals.prompt: Update the version field in package.json from 1.0.0 to 2.0.0. Show the correct way to do in-place editing with jq.
evals.expected_output: Uses jq with temp file pattern since jq lacks -i: jq '.version = "2.0.0"' package.json > package.json.tmp && mv package.json.tmp package.json
assertions: Uses jq for the JSON modification
Uses temp file pattern (not redirecting to same file)
The write-then-move pattern is atomic and safe
Does not use sed to modify JSON

evals.prompt: I have transactions.json with an array of objects, each having category and amount fields. Group by category and calculate the total amount per category.
evals.expected_output: A jq command using group_by and map with add for aggregation.
assertions: Uses jq group_by(.category) for grouping
Uses map() with add to sum amounts per group
Produces output with category name and total
Command is syntactically valid jq

evals.prompt: Extract name and email from users.json array objects. Some users have null email fields - provide a default of 'N/A' for those.
evals.expected_output: A jq command using the alternative operator // for null handling.
assertions: Uses jq for JSON processing
Uses the alternative operator (//) for null/missing values
Provides 'N/A' as default for null email
Extracts both name and email fields

evals.prompt: Convert a JSON configuration file (config.json) to YAML format using command-line tools.
evals.expected_output: Uses dasel or yq for format conversion: dasel -f config.json -w yaml or yq -P config.json
assertions: Uses dasel or yq for conversion (not manual reformatting)
Specifies correct output format flag (-w yaml or -o yaml or -P)
Does not use echo/printf/sed for format conversion
Command produces valid YAML output

evals.prompt: I just received a new CSV file called sales.csv and need to understand its structure. Show me the columns, row count, and basic statistics.
evals.expected_output: A sequence of qsv commands: headers, count, and stats with table formatting.
assertions: Uses qsv headers to show column names
Uses qsv count for row count
Uses qsv stats for statistical summary
Pipes stats output through qsv table for readability

evals.prompt: Join orders.csv (with columns order_id, user_id, amount) with users.csv (with columns id, name, email) on the user_id/id columns to get order details with user names.
evals.expected_output: A qsv join command: qsv join user_id orders.csv id users.csv
assertions: Uses qsv join for the dataset merge
Specifies correct key columns (user_id from orders, id from users)
Files are in correct order for the join
Does not use awk or paste for joining CSVs

evals.prompt: I need to edit values in three different config files: settings.toml, config.json, and docker-compose.yml. What tools should I use for each?
evals.expected_output: Recommends dasel or format-specific tools: dasel for TOML, jq for JSON, yq for YAML.
assertions: Recommends dasel for TOML (jq/yq cannot handle TOML)
Recommends jq (or dasel) for JSON
Recommends yq (or dasel) for YAML
Does not recommend grep, sed, or awk for any format

evals.prompt: Update the container image for the 'app' container in deployment.yml to 'myapp:3.0'. The image is under .spec.template.spec.containers[] where name is 'app'.
evals.expected_output: A yq command using select to target the correct container: yq -i '(.spec.template.spec.containers[] | select(.name == "app")).image = "myapp:3.0"' deployment.yml
assertions: Uses yq for YAML editing
Uses select(.name == 'app') to target the correct container
Sets the .image field on the selected container
Uses -i for in-place editing

evals.prompt: Merge three JSON config files (base.json, staging.json, production.json) into a single combined object where later files override earlier ones.
evals.expected_output: Uses jq -s for slurp mode with object merge: jq -s '.[0] * .[1] * .[2]' base.json staging.json production.json
assertions: Uses jq with -s (slurp) flag to read multiple files
Uses the * operator for deep object merge
Files are merged in order so later files override
Does not use cat or manual concatenation

evals.prompt: I have a 2GB CSV file (logs.csv). I need to index it for fast access, take a random sample of 1000 rows, and get statistics on the sample.
evals.expected_output: Uses qsv index, qsv sample, and qsv stats for efficient large file processing.
assertions: Uses qsv index to create an index for fast access
Uses qsv sample for random sampling
Uses qsv stats on the sample for analysis
Does not attempt to load the entire file into memory

evals.prompt: Add a new Redis service to docker-compose.yml with image redis:7-alpine, port mapping 6379:6379, and a named volume redis-data:/data.
evals.expected_output: A yq -i command that adds the complete service definition.
assertions: Uses yq with -i for in-place editing
Adds the service under .services.redis
Includes image, ports, and volumes fields
Does not use echo/cat to append YAML text

evals.prompt: From items.json (an array of objects with a 'category' field), extract all unique category values and sort them alphabetically.
evals.expected_output: A jq command: jq '[.[].category] | unique | sort' items.json or jq '[.[].category] | unique' items.json (unique sorts by default).
assertions: Uses jq for JSON processing
Extracts .category from array elements
Uses unique to deduplicate values
Does not use sort/uniq shell commands on JSON output

evals.prompt: Read the project version from a Maven pom.xml file. The version is at .project.version.
evals.expected_output: A dasel command: dasel -f pom.xml '.project.version'
assertions: Uses dasel for XML reading (not grep or xmllint with xpath)
Targets the correct path .project.version
Does not use grep or regex to parse XML
Command is syntactically valid dasel

evals.prompt: Analyze the distribution of values in the 'status' column of orders.csv. Show the top 10 most frequent values with their counts.
evals.expected_output: Uses qsv frequency: qsv frequency orders.csv --select status --limit 10 | qsv table
assertions: Uses qsv frequency for value distribution
Specifies --select status for the target column
Uses --limit for top N results
Pipes through qsv table for readable output
